content:
The Older I Get, The Fewer Layers I Want Between Me and the Bug

8 min read
Just now

--

Give the same feature request to a developer with two years of experience and one with fifteen, and you’ll usually get two very different pull requests. The junior one tends to arrive with more files: a new interface, a new abstract base class, maybe a factory, definitely a service layer sitting between two other service layers. It looks thorough. It looks like effort was put in. It looks, frankly, impressive.
The senior one tends to arrive smaller. Sometimes uncomfortably smaller. A single method added to an existing class. A direct query where the junior version would have added a repository method, a service method, and an interface for both. And the senior developer, if you ask them why they didn’t “architect it properly,” will usually give you some version of the same answer: “because this doesn’t need it yet.”
This isn’t laziness, and it isn’t a lack of ambition. It’s the opposite — it’s what remains after you’ve been burned enough times by architecture that looked ambitious on day one and became unbearable by month six. This article is about why that instinct develops, what it actually optimizes for, and why “simple” is a much harder thing to produce than “impressive-looking.”
